reasoning: An album staying at #1 on the Billboard 200 for 16 consecutive weeks is an astronomically rare event. The last album to achieve 16 straight weeks at #1 was the 'Titanic' soundtrack in 1998 — not even monumental phenomenons like Taylor Swift's 'The Tortured Poets Department' (12 straight weeks) or Morgan Wallen's 'One Thing at a Time' (15 straight weeks) reached 16 consecutive weeks. While 'The Life of a Showgirl' had a historic debut of 4 million units, its consumption dropped aggressively by 95% in just two weeks, falling to 194,000 units in its third week. This steep week-over-week decay curve means the album will likely drop below 100,000 units by November. At those numbers, it will be overwhelmingly vulnerable to major November standard releases, as well as the inevitable December wave of holiday albums (like Michael Bublé's 'Christmas'), which historically surge to well over 100,000 equivalent units and reliably take over the Billboard 200 summit. Maintaining #1 for 16 weeks would require surviving untouched from October through the entire holiday season and into late January. The probability of this happening is extremely close to zero.
